content.problemStatement:
Now, let's take a deeper look at BFS.

Breadth-First Search (BFS) is a graph traversal algorithm that systematically explores a graph by visiting all the nodes at the current depth before moving on to nodes at the next depth. It starts from a source node and explores its neighbors first, before moving on to the next level of neighbors.

**Algorithm Steps:**


Start from a source node.
Enqueue the source node into a queue and mark it as visited.
While the queue is not empty:

Dequeue a node from the queue and process it (print or do other operations).
Enqueue all unvisited neighbors of the dequeued node into the queue and mark them as visited.


Repeat until the queue is empty.
Video Explanation
Task
Test the given code in editor with different graph's inputs. Also submit the code.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain two space separated integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of nodes and edges in the graph.
The next MMM lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these MMM lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​, denoting a bidirectional edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​.
constraints: The graph is undirected and unweighted
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers N and M, denoting the number of nodes and edges in the graph. The next M lines describe the edges. The i-th of these M lines contains two space-separated integers u_i and v_i, denoting a bidirectional edge between u_i and v_i.
content.outputFormat: The output will be the BFS traversal of the graph, with the nodes separated by spaces.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The graph has 4 vertices and 5 edges. The BFS traversal starts from vertex 1 and visits all its neighbors (2, 3, 4) before moving on to the next level of neighbors.
The graph has 5 vertices and 6 edges. The BFS traversal starts from vertex 1 and visits all its neighbors (2, 5) before moving on to the next level of neighbors (3, 4).
The graph has 3 vertices and 3 edges. The BFS traversal starts from vertex 1 and visits all its neighbors (2, 3) before moving on to the next level of neighbors.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm. BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that systematically explores a graph by visiting all the nodes at the current depth before moving on to nodes at the next depth. We start from a source node, enqueue it into a queue, and mark it as visited. Then, we dequeue a node from the queue, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors into the queue and mark them as visited. We repeat this process until the queue is empty.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + M) per test case, where N is the number of nodes and M is the number of edges. This is because we visit each node and edge once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the queue and visited array.
